text: Welcome to This is my Architecture. Today we are in Zurich, Switzerland, and I'm here with Marco
from Codebit. Hi Marco. Hello, thanks for having me today. Marco, can you tell us about Codebit and
Elpro? Of course. So Codebit is an AWS advanced consulting partner based in Zurich, Switzerland.
Elpro is a temperature monitoring solution company based in Books, Switzerland. They are mainly
working with pharmaceutical companies that need to have goods shipped and temperature controlled.
Interesting. And how is Codebit helping Elpro on their cloud journey? So Elpro has rewritten
their software, the Elpro cloud software, a couple of years ago. So they actually have embraced the
12 factor principles and have written their software in a way that is fully stateless,
modularized, containerized, and actually want to put that on a very lean architecture.
Great. Then let's have a look at the actual architecture.
Sure. We need to start at the beginning. So that means we first had to create a landing zone.
A landing zone was created to actually secure the environment for them. So we put in CloudTrail,
GuardDuty, Config, and a couple of Lambdas to secure automatically their accounts. And then we started
to work on the networking part. So we used the Transit Gateway to actually create a networking hub where all the
connections are coming in. Connections that we are feeding in are from IoT sensors connected to
providers via 3G connectivity. And those providers are 3G and 4G connectivity. And actually these providers
are having a side-to-side VPN connection and an attachment to the Transit Gateway. So that all the accounts can be reached.
The admin users, they are actually coming in via Client VPN because sometimes they work remote. So they are
coming in via secure Client VPN connectivity. And Client VPN again is then connected to the Transit Gateway.
So that's the groundwork for the networking that we have delivered to them.
Wow. That looks like some very solid groundwork that you have built. Now, what about the actual application?
As mentioned before, the application is fully containerized. So we can put it on ECS Fargate.
ECS Fargate is a really scalable, easy to use container orchestration solution where we can have all the
microservices stored and executed. And the APIs that are consumed are actually exposed via an ALB.
And then the actual end users that need to consume these REST APIs, they are coming in as well via the ALB.
The end user interface is actually a React application.
Okay.
The React single page application is stored on an S3 bucket. So super lean as well.
And that S3 bucket is then connected to CloudFront, which gives them a CDN distribution worldwide in a secure way to the end users.
So they are consuming the single page application out of CloudFront.
Great. So you have a Fargate here, which is basically serverless containers.
I'm interesting to know, how do you add the data and persistence to those containers?
Yes. Even if the application is very stateless, some containers still use state.
And that is actually stored via an EFS connectivity. So they are accessing files and storing files on EFS,
which is a fully managed service from AWS. And that is going via the EFS protocol.
And then the database is actually used in Postgres mode. So they are storing all the data that
these sensors are producing at the calculations in a Postgres running on Aurora database.
Aurora is actually using the Graviton CPUs, which gives them an excellent price performance ratio.
That's great to hear. So this sounds like a very solid and lean architecture that you have built.
Now, how many accounts do you have? How many environments does Elpro use?
Yes, actually they're using a lot of environments and a lot of accounts.
So when we started to build the whole thing, we started in the development infrastructure account.
So there we started to define in a declarative way all the services that AWS is using. After that was done
and tested from Elpro, we started to roll that out in a programmatic way in multiple accounts.
Development account, demo account, production account, and in the future, even private builds will be
done in separate environments for large pharmaceutical companies.
Good. So I think that Infrastructure Score is helping you a lot on that.
That's absolutely right. So Infrastructure as Code is very important for them because they have so
many accounts, they need to look the same. And with Infrastructure as Code, we can have the templates
and the parameter file store in a Git repository and can very easily consume and enhance those files for the future.
Cool. Now, what will the future bring to Elpro?
Actually, Elpro's business is booming. So they're transporting, they're helping pharmaceutical companies,
transporting a lot of vaccines. So really booming. With that, they have a lot of sensors sending in data.
So a lot more data that is coming in, but also more consumers of the APIs are exposed.
And even some consolidation of legacy applications have started to onto a common cohesive platform.
Because they have now everything built on a very solid groundwork with CloudFormation,
and a lot of automation built in, they don't need to babysit the environment so often anymore.
So they get now a lot of free time to build business value, we're building new features for Elpro themselves.
That's fantastic. Thanks a lot, Marco, for sharing your architecture with us today.
Sure. Thank you.
And thanks for watching. This is my architecture.
